Note: I received these products at a discount in exchange for my honest review, which I am happy to provide:

First, I'd like to point out how great the seller was. Not only were the items shipped lightning-fast and securely packaged, but the seller sent me a shipping confirmation email with a note to let them know if I had any problems or questions. Further, the package contained a card noting that these items come with a limited warranty, as well as a coupon for 20% off at their shop.

Second, I'd also like to point out that you don't need to be into beading to consider purchasing this kit. It is a great thing to add to your tool box or wherever for handling all those little repairs, adjustments, and the like to your jewelry, such as adding a charm to a bracelet. The bead board is likewise handy for tasks like restringing your pearls (my first project). I also will use it as a layout design tool for experimenting with ideas.

The compact tool kit case has a sturdy zipper. It opens flat for easy access while working and provides a spot to keep your tools so they don't end up disappearing under other stuff. There is room in the kit for me to add my small jewelry rasps. There are a surprising number of tools tucked into the stretchy-band holders of the case, including: Wire cutter; straight and bent-tip tweezers; a gadget that I am unfamiliar with but can immediately see the benefit of (like an awl with a brace that pops in and out); Exacto knife; straight and curved beading needles; clip scissors; different tipped needle-nose pliers; and a thimble (not the regular kind, but looks useful for beading projects). It's great to have such complete set so easy to hand when needed.

While not tiny, the compact size of the tools are great for my small hands and I imagine they would work well for children too. For a casual user like me, these tools seem sturdy enough. I am unsure whether the slender awl or wire cutter could handle overly enthusiastic applications, but that's not what they are meant for.

The beading board is made of molded plastic, with neutral grey flocking on the working side to control slipping and rolling. The board has assorted measured, numbered and notched markings, including a three-layer section, as well as one for measuring up to 34". There are several wells for holding beads and findings, including a large center well, and smaller corner wells. I am light-handed and not prone to breaking things, so this board is sturdy enough for me. However, if you are the type of person who breaks things just by looking at them, or if you have children, then it is possible that this board might be flimsy enough to crack. Even if so, it would be simple enough to repair it with tape on the back.

Besides restringing pearls, I plan to use the bead board to explore new design ideas to repurpose some old costume jewelry. I think this set could be a fun thing for a grandmom/mom/daughter kind of afternoon project, or similar ways for friends and family to pass the time together and re-use all the old jewelry they have sitting around.

I love these sorts of large cross stitch projects. They take me months to complete, but it's a labor of love.

This particular pattern seems to have enough thread for every color and little for mistakes. I normally do the 11 count patterns and this one is 14 count. The stitches are smaller and on a pattern this big, it's really noticable and my eyes get tired. Still, the finished product should be beautiful.

I do a lot of cross stitch work and finding good needles is always a problem. These are pretty decent. They don't break in half when i make a bad set of stitches. The larger eyes are definitely easier to thread.

I stitched this lovely Rhodesian Ridgeback as a gift for a friend and it took about 8 weeks from start to finish in the evenings after work. The pattern is easy to see and follow and washed away cleanly. They included enough thread (floss) for every color and I ended up with a full strand left in almost every color. 90% of the thread pulled apart easily on the first try with almost no snagging or waste. The finished piece is really beautiful and definitely worth framing. The photo is at 80% finished. This a large pattern.

I hate those cheap, thin needles that come in the cross stitch kits. They always snap in half and the eyes are tiny. These needles are much better.

They are slightly thicker so you can actually get a grip on the thing. The eyes are easy to see and less aggravating to thread. So far, I've have not had one break.

I love these scissors. I had been using an old pair of nail clippers for my cross stitch threads and it worked fine. However, these scissors are better.

They are quite sharp and cut cleanly and evenly. The finger holes are big enough that the scissors don't get stuck on my hand - always a plus. The compact size means they fit in my needlework kit bag without any drama.

Great price for these acrylic paint pens. I also like the various colors that come in this set, especially the white one, which is really what I needed for my project. These pens will definitely come in very handy.
Expected sturdier  paper folded for card-making. Pretty hefty price for even 50 lightweight odd-sized pieces of paper that look torn & singed on the edges.  Pretty disappointed.
Just one side of this product is coated. Backside is regular burlap & any contact with moisture renders it unusable.
Pricey pens, so you don't  expect one to begin leaking after a couple of uses.

Ingenious!  Wish I had invented this product--(oh, and Velcro). Day or night, makes stitches more visible, especially when working with some of the newer novelty yarns. Suggest lighted versions of larger hooks for bulkier yarn be released ASAP!
This tape does the job! Its not as thick as flextape. But it does the job for hanging lite stuff. Or tapeing thing's down! I do recommended this tape. I'm already finding lot's of use for this tape;

These patches work very good. Sticks fast an stays stuck. Colors are nice. I cut these into strips. An used them to bond the splits in my maxi dresses together. I brought hen tape, but the tape is on both sides. Didn't feel like looking for one sided tape. So I got these patches. You just turn the dress inside out. Cut patches into strips. Put the split together. An iron them on the inside of the dress. So now my maxi dresses have no side splits. Very happy with these patches.
They are cheap and sloppily made there is a huge gap inbetween the blades so have fun trying to cut anything

These are mini hex drivers and it seems like they made them one at a time with a grinder or something as they are so out of shape the smallest 1.5 stripped out first try and the larger one the handle is too small to even turn. Just spend another 5 dollars and get a good hex driver.
Several plastic ruler forms came together.  Works perfectly for finding where to place a picture for heat pressing onto an item that needs to be centered.  I highly recommend.  Thank you.
This is definite necessity for any knitter who has to follow a chart or keep track of a pattern. Nice an sturdy. The magnets are very strong.

I ALWAYS need more light when sewing.
But this little lamp just doesn't put out enough oomph to help. Cute idea, but did not help in real life.

This is a decent quality double-sided satin ribbon. You get a generous amount. The spool is full, versus those spools that have a big styrofoam ring in the middle to try to look like a full spool.

The quality is great for crafting. This would work very well for ornaments, doll clothes, hanging loops, etc. I would definitely buy this one again.

I bought these to use as supports for wooden circles my daughter and I plan to paint for holiday decorations. I'll be gluing these to the backs of the circles. I am not sure how strong they will be when it gets windy, but we'll find out!

Overall, happy with the purchase.

These are going to be very cute for some outdoor holiday decorating. They are bright, large enough to be versatile and are a good shade of red.

BUT, beware the glitter monster! These are loaded with glitter. This is what makes them great for outside usage...leave the mess outside!
I'll post photos when we are done.

This is a set of stencils. They are much larger than I thought they would be, based on mfg photos.

There is a really nice assortment of designs. Each stencil has a hole in one upper corner. There is a ring included, so the stencils can be put on the ring if desired. I sure don't recommend that, unless you can hang them up on a pegboard or something similar. Stencils need to be stored flat so they don't get bends or warped.

I haven't had the chance to actually try them yet, but don't forsee many issues. Most of the designs with cobwebs were messed up, with the cobwebs twisted or broken out.

Overall, this is a nice assortment of Halloween designs that would adapt to several types of craft projects.

Anyone who sews or does needlework can appreciate a pair of snips like these. It is much easier to go in and snip threads with a pair like this vs a pair of full scissors.

They are a little stiff, but will hopefully loosen up with use. The blades are nice and sharp. It will be helpful with my long list of projects coming up.

This was just as I had hoped, and perfect for craft projects.

The faux leather is very flexible. It was easy to cut with sharp scissors. I have tried gluing it on a project (I used E6000 glue, not regular craft glue, because of the thickness), and I have sewed it onto a sweatshirt. I used a jeans needle and a puece of tissue paper on top of the faux leather, and had no problems.

I would definitely buy this again.

I love the Santee blender fabrics. The sunny is no exception.

These have a bit of a stiff hand at first, but washing and drying takes care of that. They don't have huge color bleeds (I use color catchers in the wash anyway), and sew up very well. Very happy with this fabric.

I love Henry Glass fabric designs. They have created some of my favorite fabrics.

This panel is just beautiful. It has one of my favorite birds - cardinals. I am going to have to figure out how to best use them for a quilt.

Love it, and happy I bought them.

This is a hand-held circle punch.
The circle is 2" across.
While mostly plastic, this punch has "heft" to it, due to the great metal shape parts.

It gives a nice, clean punch without enormous effort or hand strength. You can use both the outline and the punches themselves. The punched out circles collect under the punch in a shallow tray with a sliding cover. No more chasing your punchies all over, or picking them up off the floor. I tested ours with cardstock weight paper, and it did a great job.

I really like these punches. They are great quality and have handled anything needed. My daughter loves this circle one for scrapbooking. They make a quick way to create a photo accent.

This has a very nice hand to it. While it has a gold metallic outline on some items, it is not glitter and doesn't shed.

I have this fabric in both this, the cream background, and the black background, and love them both. The scale fits in well for many designs.

I was not sure what to expect with a random "bag of scraps". As a crafter, that can mean anything from postage stamp size on up.

I was happily surprised by both the quantity and the size of the scraps. This was all white long pile faux fur. The pieces were wonderfully big, and useful for nearly any project. If you make gnomes, these scraps are great for beards.

The fur is nice and soft, and with a polyester background. It should be easy to sew or glue onto various projects. I'm very happy with this item.

I originally bought this because it was the only fabric in the color I needed.
But I was pleasantly surprised by its softness. It worked well for my project, a rag quilt. I have since bought this in other colors.

Many times, people think any thread will do, because it is all the same. This is far from the truth. Not only does thread quality affect your finished product, it also affects your sewing experience.

Different brands of machines seem to have their own thread preferences. There are some that just do not work with my Brother sewing machine. All they do is fray, snap or tangle (and tension has nothing to do with performance in this case). It makes sewing stressful instead of fun.

After much trial and error with at least eight brands of thread, I have found that my Brother machine LOVES Aurifil thread. It runs like a charm, no snapping or tangling, no stress! While it is a bit pricey, it has been worth it. I don't have any hesitation in recommending Aurifil thread. And they have a very large color collection - you'll be able to find just what you need for your projects.

These are not top of the line paintbrushes. But they certainly do an adequate job for most acrylic uses. They cleaned up fairly easily, even though they seemed to suck more paint into the ferrel area than most brushes I've used. They didn't leave hair or brush strokes in the paint, which is sometimes the best you can hope for.
Great for crafting.

This is a very handy kit to keep near your sewing machine. It contains all the basics to help you change out feet or attachments, and adjust bobbin cases.
It also has a pair of snips for quick thread catches.

Additionally, there is a pair of needlenose tweezers, a fringe brush and a small bottle brush, great for getting lint out from under the bobbin area.

Are they the top of the line of everything? No, but more than adequate. My only suggestion is that I wish they had come in a small snap-close plastic case instead of a zip-close bag, but that is minor.

If you use a sewing machine (any brand), this kit is great to have within reach.

[[VIDEOID:eb09b7e12a0f8a328a3965dfbbf5b8a6]]
We've never had an actual belt punch before. My do-it-yourself husband would take a nail and hammer to make extra loops when needed. It worked, but the holes were kinda ugly! Ha!

This comes with the main mechanism, a few extra plate caps, and hole borer (to clean up any leftover mess from the punch) and a case to keep everything neatly together.

This product worked very well. It was fairly intuitive, but I still read the directions. Crazy, huh.

The strangest part was the little plastic caps you leave on the bottom portion. I guess that is to prevent a repetitive dent in the strike plate.

It is easy to dial to the size hole you need. We had no problems at all punching new holes in a couple of old belts. I think the listing description of "super heavy duty" goes a little too far, but definitely can handle most of what you would need at home.

Overall, very pleased with this item. I hope we find other uses for it as well.

I am pretty critical of most embroidery scissors, because most these days are mass-produced, cheap, heavy messes.

This pair was actually comfortable to use. I could open them easily without having to "pull" them open, which was great for hands with arthritis. The finger openings are a little bit small, so these will not be the best choice for men or ladies with larger hands.

The blades seemed to cut well without pulling or tugging. I had no current project going, so I tried them out by trimming some tags off of old washcloths. They did just fine. I think they would work fine for most sewing/embroidery projects. Are they the sharpest or best? Nope. But, they will work just fine for the majority of sewing needs.

I was disappointed by how little yarn you actually get. I thought I was buying one of the large skeins based on the photo. What I received was so small it fit in my hand.

I've used Red Heart for many years. It isn't the top quality, but certainly great for many projects. These days, I make crocheted head coverings to hand out to people in need. Red Heart does fine for that. I just thought I was getting a much larger skein, so be sure to read descriptions carefully.

Like so many people, I need a little help these days threading needles.
This is a little plastic case with needle threaders made of plastic and wire.

The threaders are like a flat, plastic oval for easy fitting by your thumb. If you've ever seen a worry stone, it is like that, only plastic. There is a tiny wire for putting your thread through. Overall, they are just fine, but I'll stick with my little aluminum threaders...the eye to put your thread through is much larger on the aluminum ones.

I have done counted cross stitch for many, many years, but it had been quite a while since starting a new project.

What I learned quickly is how much my vision has changed! The 18-count Aida cloth is a bit small for my preference, but I will make it work! It is just going to take longer. I thought this was a two-afternoon project, but it is going to be a two-week project for me. Haha! But that's fine.

The gnome is adorable and the colors are bright and cheerful. I would recommend this kit.
This is a nice batch of dyed ostrich feathers. The feathers are soft and the dye is nice and even. These would work for nearly any craft application.

[[VIDEOID:ec6fe9b642fc43a6f744bab116080183]] I sew, craft and quilt. Scissors are a very important part of every project. Fabric scissors especially need to be of good quality and easy to use.

This is a set: one pair of black scissors and one pair of thread snips. I've never had a thread snip, but long wanted one. I'll need to keep looking.

The scissors are also disappointing.
Of course, I've used scissors for many decades, and quickly know the good vs bad. But as I have gotten older, my hands have a touch of arthritis in them, and it accentuates the flaws in scissors that aren't up to snuff.

I did a mini video on this set. The snips drag and catch at the back end, which makes my teeth stand on edge.

The scissors do have one redeeming quality...the finger openings are HUGE. This might make it a good thing for the men out there who like to sew (and there are plenty). Along with large handle openings, these things are heavy - way too heavy to be comfortable in my hand. If they worked amazingly well, I'd try to overlook the awkardness and weight, but they don't. They drag a bit, and required me forcing them back open...sewing folks will know what I mean.

I cannot recommend this set to anyone. It is just too awkward, heavy and not tooled very well.

Ah, feathers. Everyone who buys feathers has their own plans for them.
In my case, these are cat toys.

Cats usually love feathers, especially if their favorite human is at the other end of it. I chose purple, because it is one of the few colors kitties can see.

So far, they are enjoying their playtime. The feathers are a nice quality (but take them away if the end splits, or trim the split off). The cats give two paws up, and the humans are happy too.

I have been wanting a nylon sewing foot for my Brother machine, so was glad to see this one being offered. (Note that most feet are NOT machine-specific, only high-shank or low-shank. This foot does fit Brother machines despite not being listed.)

It arrived very quickly. I do have some concerns with the finish on the bottom. It looks very roughly finished. I have not had the chance to sew with this yet, but I will DEFINITELY be trying it on scrap fabric before using it on an actual project, to make sure this is not going to snag my fabric.

This set of paintbrushes is not your high-end artiste brushes. However, if you do crafting with acrylic paints, this set will work very well for you.

We had no problems with using them or cleaning them. So far, they are not leaving bristles behind, and no problem with stiff hair marks in the paint.

This is a good set for basic painting needs. I would say they are best suited for use with acrylics.

I love markers. If you like to create things, you know you can't have too many. But, all markers are not created equal!

These have a nice color assortment. Super easy to use and are nicely opaque. I would not necessarily call them "metallic"...they are more like a frosted color (think frost nail polish).
They were smooth and easy to use.

I do have one complaint about them, however. Hopefully, people who draw a lot will understand what I mean when I say these leave liquid "blobs" at every starting and stopping point. I find that really annoying. When you start and/or stop, it leaves an extra little circle of liquid. Ugh. That's fine if you are doing a particular kind of lettering, but it is something you don't want for most projects.

So, these are okay for adult coloring books or kids' projects, but I definitely cannot recommend them and would never repurchase these.

We recently received our package of wood slices for crafting. I've always wanted to try them but never had the chance.

I am happy to share with readers that these have a great quality to them. They are nice and thick, and feel sturdy in your hands. The bark is attractive as well. Each one comes pre-drilled with a hole large enough to accommodate nearly any hanging method. It comes with a small bundle of jute, and a similar bundle of orange and natural twine. You could easily substitute any color twine to complement your projects, or use grosgrain or organza ribbon as well.

The slices themselves have a beautiful, smooth finish on them...no real prep needed for most mediums. You can use wood burning, any kind of paint, or even markers on these. I think I would seal them when projects are completed, not due to the wood quality, but to preserve your artwork.

I am very happy with these, and look forward to trying out several media types on them.

Kaufman Kona is always a nice quality of fabric.
I have a color chart of their items, and it is always exactly on without variances.

Note that Kona is a relatively low-thread-count fabric. Some folks like it just fine, but
others think it is too loose to use for quilting.

I use this for craft projects. While it frays pretty easily, it is a tight enough weave to not have any other colors show through.I have a lot of Kona fabrics, because I love the color choices.

I have only been quilting a couple of years, but the Omnigrip rulers have quickly become my favorite rulers.
They are a little pricey compared to others, but they are worth it.
The measurements are accurate (you would be surprised to find out how many cheap rulers are off a little bit in their measurements!),
and the neon color underneath has been visible on every fabric I've ever used.
They are made from heavy acrylic and have tolerated whatever I've given them, from heavy use, rotary cutters and dropping.

They will likely be my forever favorite brand of crafting ruler.
I highly recommend any of the Omnigrip products.

I love the Warm Product line products.
I already had used several of the other items, and had the Warm and Natural
sitting on the shelf.
However, I had a project that was using a white-on-white fabric, and my Warm and Natural
was showing through. So, I decided to see if there was really a difference with the Warm and White.

Well, there was! It was a very nice white that did not alter the colors of the fabric on top.
My white fabric looked even better with the Warm and White behind it.
The quality is the normal high quality Warm Products.

I would definitely recommend any of the Warm Products for quilters.
If you don't like those little "hairs" of batting sticking up on the top side of your fabric ,
then go with this brand in cotton

This was shipped quickly, and fabric was new (not used being passed off as new).
It is hairy and furry, of course, but very lightweight, not heavy and bulky.

I wanted it for a craft purpose, so it will be perfect.
If you want to WEAR it, well, it is lightweight! Ha!

But... this stuff sheds like a giant critter.
After I cut off a section for my project, it looked like a small dog
had exploded on the floor! It is MESSY to cut!

But overall, very pleased with the quality and hand of the fabric.
I would buy it again if I run out, and would try it in different colors.

We finally got the chance to use this thread. We had it about a month before our enbroidery machine arrived.

The thread is a wonderful way to get a good color assortment, to find out what you will really use and which ones you don't.
You can then buy larger spools of your favorite colors.

We did have one thread break on us - that was a bit of a pain, because our machine wouldn't let us rethread just that thread - we had to start the entire design over on a new piece of fabric.

All in all, not the top of the line, but a very decent basic thread, and the color assortment is a really great starting place.

I love to do crafts of all kinds, and know how to hand-embroider. I've always wanted to own an embroidery machine, but could not afford one. I was a little bit skeptical at first, since my first experience with Brother was a lemon sewing machine (it only truly likes to work in reverse!). Compared to other machines, the price point on this one just cannot be beat!

The manual is more than a little intimidating. Some of it is very roughly translated-to-English descriptions, but I muddled through to the point I was willing to give it an actual try. Putting the parts together was much worse on paper than in real life - a simple snap-on to the bottom part, and then snap on the hoop and I was ready to roll. On to the pleasant option of which design to try first.

My experience with the automatic needle threader was more frustration than helpful, and I found myself wishing I could take it off so I could reach the #6 threading spot easier. The instructions seem to overcomplicate it, so I'll try it again in another way viewed on a video and see if I don't want to take a hammer to it.

On the embroidery - you do have to change the thread a lot to get the desired results. Thankfully, like most Brother machines, the threading sequence is printed right on the machine so you aren't trying to remember if the thread goes over or under that metal piece or around this little piece. We used a rayon type thread, which has a nice sheen to it. Since I was giving the machine a test run, we did our tests on simple (translation: cheap) muslin fabric with tear-away stabilizer on the back. I have included some pictures on our results. I wish we could load more photos, but I guess that would overload the website. (I also tried to take a video, but failed miserably for some reason.) I would advise users to buy the best thread you can afford, because one of our cheaper colors caused the needle to break because of thread burrs hitting the eye of the needle. SNAP!!
That was the one actual complaint I had about this machine - we had to stop and put on a new needle of course, and we were on Color #3 of a six-color design. We could not go BACK to Color #3 and continue - we had to scrap the hoop contents and start over from the beginning. I would have liked the option to continue where we left off, but couldn't.

The embroidery is not perfection - there were little gaps between the colored thread and the outlines on a couple designs we tried, but nothing overly bad. If I wasn't fine-tooth-combing it, I might not even notice. We even tried one of the six-color designs in only ONE color, just to see how it would look (and to save thread!) - it looked surprisingly good! For those new to machine embroidery, the machine will have connecting threads by color - in other words, if there are five areas that use black, there will be thread running between those five areas. It is normal and perfectly ok. This machine starts with a few anchoring stitches - just trim away the connected threads - they are NOT part of your design. That goes for the front AND the back.

There are several Disney and Pixar licensed designs included with this model. There are five fonts as well. I wish there were more, and you can bet that fonts are the FIRST thing I am going to look for to download! Yes, you can just download more options, designs, fonts and frames to this machine. Well, to your computer, then transfer them to your machine via USB port. I have also learned as of today that there is software available to even digitize your own designs, which will create truly custom designs for your machine. The Brother uses files in the PES format (there are a LOT of embroidery formats out there, so be sure you stick with the right type for the machine). Brother has included their design library website location on the front of the machine, apparently knowing they will be creating an insatiable thirst for more designs and fonts!

There are many YouTube videos available on how to use this machine, in case you are not into reading roughly translated manuals (even though I recommend at minimum familiarizing yourself with the machine via the manuals before trying using it). I've been looking at some today, and have already learned some new tricks.

All in all, I truly love this machine. It does everything you could want (except large designs - that will take a different model - the maximum embroidery area for this one is 4x4, even though the hoop is larger than that) and does it very nicely. I am thrilled and extremely grateful to have had the chance to own it! In fact, since trying it out, my mind has been racing as to all the things I could make (or try to make) with this, and I haven't done a lick of actual work. My daughter also tried out a design and had no trouble with it either. We are in the process of making her first quilt, and had been wondering how we could get some of the words she wanted to include on her fabric as part of the design. Now we know! We'll be creating our own blocks.

I seriously can recommend this machine to anyone who enjoys making custom gifts or even wants to start selling embroidered items as a side business. This is definitely for home use - the size and speed are not going to work for a high-volume business. But this home user is incredibly happy with the machine, and can barely wait to try some new fonts! You WILL enjoy this one!

Note that I've read more than one review that complained about nesting on the back. If you have not done any sort of machine embroidery before, please know that you HAVE to use some sort of stabilizer fabric on the back of the item on which you are embroidering. Amazon sells many different types. I like to buy the precut, since it saves me a lot of time and frustration. This machine uses 8"x8" precuts. Any type will do, but I like the tear-away the best so far. If you are using a stretchy fabric, you'll want a more permanent stabilizer. Also, cheap threads are not a friend to this machine. That broken needle was on my first time out - the cheap thread caused a burr that snapped the needle in half. Get the best thread you can afford.

Good luck, and happy embroidering!

I use a lot of pins in my crafts, and this was a great way to get them - a big ol' honking box of pins!
There is not a lot you can really say about straight pins, but I'll try.

They have a coating on them, so they will not rust, leaving stains on your items or clothing.
There is also a little bag of damp-absorber in each box, so there is no condensation in the box from the metals.

I've been using this brand for about a year now, and have not found one that I like better. Prym pins are perfect for my crafting needs.

I have purchased these many. many times now.
I really love them! The manufacturer even puts a little packet of dessicant in there so you don't get
rusty pins. I appreciate that. If it matters, they are very shiny silver, not dull like some pins.
You can pick these up with a magnet, so that is also helpful, since I often drop pins!

The only things I would change, is to make the heads flatter. They are slightly rounded, which makes for a slightly
lifted appearance on some of my projects. You can't really get them flattened out. I also wish that this brand sold 7/8" pins in bulk, but I have not found any place that sells them yet. My Amazon seller does not offer that size.

Saw these hooks and decided to give them a try. I'm older and I've developed fairly severe osteoarthritis in my hands so finding ergonomic hooks that work has been a bit tricky. Some that say "ergonomic" might work for others, but most of the time, there's just something "off" that causes problems for me.

These are the first hooks I've found that I can use painlessly without having to adjust my hold or hand position. The handles are longer which helps with balance. And the shape of the handles also works better (slightly flattened near the hook end; fuller/rounder near the "handle" end). I use the "knife" hold (rather than the "pencil" hold) so that may make a difference. All I know is that I can use these for several hours at a time without any pain or pressure.

The range of sizes is great -- I can handle everything from bulky weight yarns for winter scarves to fine sock yarn for ... well ... socks.

No complaints at all -- very happy with these! Solid 5 stars.

I purchased this to hold my hair accessories: scrunches, head bands, elastics, barrettes/clips. I have waist-length hair and use a lot if these sorts of things to keep the hair out of my face. So far, it’s perfect for that.

PROs:
Compartments are big enough to hold several scrunchies in each so I can sort by color
Each bin has removable dividers so I can configure the layout to match what I need to store
Doesn’t take up a lot of space

CONS
Not the sturdiest version of this design — great for relatively light-weight items, but not for heavier stuff (I.e. hardware/bolts/nuts)
Not too sure about overall durability. Mine is fine right now — the side latches snap into place easily and hold securely, but after a few hundred uses? I plan to be gentle with it to prolong the life of the latches.

I’m giving it 5 stars because it really does work perfectly for my use (see photos). It wasn’t very expensive so if I get several years of use, I’ll be very happy.

I love doing embroidery, but as I've aged, I've developed arthritis in the joints in my fingers making it difficult to find a thimble that fits well enough to work without hurting that first joint.

This one is PERFECT! Honestly, it has a soft feel to it but is sturdy enough to handle my embroidery needle (I do mostly embroidery plus some counted cross stitch). It fits over that enlarged joint with no problems -- snug enough to feel secure, but "soft" enough not to hurt when I use it.

Very happy with this! Highly recommended if you're looking for a thimble for larger knuckles.

I'm relatively new to diamond painting, and someone suggested I use a larger drill tray to help get the drills lined up.  I chose this set sort of randomly (honestly, they all look the same to me) because I liked the combination of the "funnel opening' trays and the "closed 'gate'" version.  The price was right, too.  I've been using them for about a week and so far have noticed no issues at all.  They lay flat and are easy to "shake" to get the drills to organize themselves into neat lines.  Would happily purchase again.

These were easy to put together except for the spots where the adhesive was missing -- usually around 10-15 "spots" on each piece. But they are very pretty and impressive when finished.

They don't take a huge amount of time, and I found I sort of got into a "rhythm" in placing the small stones onto the base. It was very relaxing (I worked on them when I was recovering from pulmonary emboli and didn't have a lot of energy).

Very nice designs -- lots of images of matryoshka dolls and Swedish houses.  Nice variety of images from very intricate to more "open."  My only issue is with all the Kaisercraft coloring books: the images are printed double-sided, so if you use alcohol markers (Copics, BIC MarkIts, etc.), they'll bleed through making the image on the other side of the page unusable.  Use colored pencils or water-based markers (and even with these, be careful) if you want to be able to color both sides of a page.  Otherwise, I really do like this book.

Very pretty daisy trim.  I cut them apart and use them on handcrafted greeting cards.  They're relatively "flat," so they don't add too much "thickness" to my cards, making mailing them a little less problematic (the cancellation machines in the post office don't like things that add bulk as they get caught in the machinery).  The colors are great -- perfect for my needs.  I've had other similar daisy trims that weren't nearly as nice as this one.  Will purchase again when needed.

Wasn't sure what to expect with this -- I make handcrafted greeting cards and use a lot of designer paper packs from various "premium" lines, so I figured this would be significantly lower quality.

I'm happy to say I was wrong. No, it's not quite up to the much higher-priced paper packs, but it is MUCH nicer than I expected. The colors are vibrant and each page is a different pattern (single sided, printed on one side only; reverse is white). I'm using it for some fairly simple designs for "everyday" cards - a basic matted front panel, a smaller matted highlight panel, and a sentiment label finished with a bit of ribbon/bow or a small layered flower or butterfly. Easy to assemble, and these bright colors mean that I can produce a nicely coordinated set easily. My cards are 4.25" x 5.5" -- these papers are 9" x 12" -- so I can get at least 4 cards out of each sheet.

I wish they offered other packs -- maybe pastels or neutrals in similar patterns. Very nice and definitely a bargain at less than five dollars.
I purchased this to use in my greeting card designs as accents -- mattes for labels, strips to highlight pattern changes, etc.  It has substantial weight and is easy to manipulate.  Nice colors, too.

I'm very happy with these papers. I make a lot of handcrafted greeting cards, so I tend to go for smaller prints. These papers work great. The print lends itself well to the smaller requirements of a 4.25" x 5.5" card. The colors are wonderful -- easily to coordinate with solid color cardstock. There are twelve single-sided sheets, each with a different pattern (no repeats). I can usually get 4-6 cards from each sheet depending on the layout.

I may order another pack as I think I'll be using these a lot over the next few months. Very nice -- worth five stars for sure.

I've done several "diamond dot"-type projects over the past few years. Some kits are better than others in terms of quality of the canvas, the consistency of the dots, the tools, etc. This is one of the better quality kits I've used. I had a couple of small "rivers" to deal with but it didn't detract from the quality or experience.

The symbols are clear and easy to see and the provided tools do a good job with placing the round dots in place. As soon as I get home from my travels, I'll be looking for more patterns from this brand. Very happy with it.

Confession: I already owned the big version of this - Vogue Knitting: The Ultimate Knitting Book - so I already sort of knew what to expect from this. I was curious to see what they would include/eliminate in order to come up with a "quick" version.

The good news is that the essential basics are all included: basic and advanced techniques including basic casting on, entrelac, center-out, brioche, etc.; correcting errors, finishing techniques, designing, tools, etc. Even better, it's in a packable size.

I'm doing a lot of traveling these days, mostly longer cruises (28+ days) with lots of relaxing "sea days" (moving from one port to another) where I have time to work on projects in a totally relaxed setting. I consider myself an "intermediate" knitter -- I've tackled Aran patterns and Fair Isle, but I don't knit enough to consider myself any kind of "expert." This kind of book appeals to me because I often go months between knitting projects and may forget how to do a particular stitch or technique. At my level, this little book supplies what I need to answer more basic questions and even a few more advanced.

It's packable, too -- relatively lightweight and smaller in size so I can just slip it into my project bag.

Really, no quibbles with this at all.

I've done several of these "diamond painting" pictures over the past couple of years and I'm always pleased with the results. It's not difficult -- it's kind of like "paint by number." You get a printed canvas that looks a lot like a counted cross stitch diagram coated with a sticky coating, a set of little packets of "diamonds" (little plastic gem-like bits), and the tools to place the plastic bits onto the sticky surface. You place the plastic bits one by one based on matching the color numbers, and as you work the picture emerges. It does require some dexterity and decent vision -- I use stronger reading glasses when working on one of these -- but the results can be stunning.

The level of detail varies depending on the size of the canvas. This one is what I'd call "medium" detail -- you can make out the design, but some of the super-fine details are lost due to the size of the "diamond" bits.

Overall, though, this is a fun project, goes quickly once you get the "rhythm" of placing the "diamonds," and produces a nice picture suitable for framing. I'm planning to take another of these designs along on a cruise I have planned later this year with a lot of "sea days" -- days sailing from port to port. It will be nice to have something nice to show for that time spent at sea.

Bottom line: I like this pattern a lot -- colors are vibrant and it's easy to put together. Solid 4 stars from me.

I do quite a lot of "fussy cutting": creating 3-D decoupage focals from several prints of the same pattern where I cut different layers and put them together with thin double-sided foam tape. This means I need scissors that are capable of the tiny detailed cuts necessary to produce the effects I want.

These EK Success Cutter Bee scissors are the perfect solution. They're small enough to make those fine detailed cuts, but fit my hand beautifully. They're nicely balanced, too, which makes control much easier.

Definitely a great buy -- have purchased another pair to have as a spare when/if needed.

I use these for a variety of needlework -- mostly crochet.  Many of the patterns I work have complex overlay stitches where it's imperative to "mark" a specific stitch on a previous row, sometimes more than one at a time.  These work just fine for that.  You get a LOT of them in a nice plastic case, small enough to toss into my project bag.  I like that there are different colors so that I can color-code the stitches I need to "mark."  The quality is fine -- they're not too stiff, some of them are a little "rough," but it doesn't cause any problems with size 10 thread (or fingering yarn).  Would purchase again if I need more.

These Clover Soft Touch Steel Crochet Hooks are the ONLY hooks I use. I have fairly severe osteoarthritis in my hands, and I think I've tried every "ergonomic" hook available. This is the only one that I can use relatively pain-free.

I work mostly with thread these days, and the 1.75mm hook is the one I use for highly textured patterns (lots of overlay stitches) using size 10 thread, as it keeps the thread a little "looser" than my usual 1.50mm hook. These are extremely well made, and come with a "sheath" to protect the tip for storage.

I own most of the sizes available for thread (I work mostly in size 10, but venture into size 20 and 40 occasionally). I've never noticed any issues in terms of consistent quality -- they're all excellent! Highly recommended ...

I don't consider myself particularly "artistic," but I am "crafty" in that I do a lot of crafty things (paper crafts, thread/lace crochet, etc.). I've been seeing 3D pens for awhile so I was anxious to try my hand at one. Bottom line: this is a good solid performer if you want to try your hand at this new-ish art/craft form.

The package includes everything you need to get started: pen, transformer, filament, mini-screwdriver, mini-spanner (used to tighten/loosen/remove the nozzle), an "unblocking tool" to help move "stuck" bits through the tip, a quick-start guide, and instructions. Everything is clear and set-up is simple. That said, this takes a LOT of practice. Doing "flat" shapes on paper isn't tricky, but doing true 3D ("Doodle in the Air") is not as easy as it looks. Just understand that all those nifty projects they show in the video? yeah, those will take some time and practice before you'll get something that looks reasonably nice.

One thing to bear in mind: the tip does get really hot. Think of a hot glue gun -- that kind of hot. I wouldn't consider this a "toy" -- in the wrong hands, it could be more than a little dangerous.

I've managed to get a few acceptable items and it's getting easier with time (I've been working with this off and on for a couple of weeks now). So far, I'm still working with the supply of filament/strands that came with the 3Doodler -- two packages of ABS, 25 strands of matte neon "Highlighter" colors, and 25 strands of matte "Essentials" red/yellow/blue/green/black. That's enough for a lot of practice. You're cautioned to use only 3Doodler replacement strands (3 mm) which, of course, are pricier than other 3 mm strands; using other strands will void the warranty. However, they do go a long way, so until other manufacturer's 3 mm strands are authorized, this may not be a huge issue.

I haven't had any issues so far (no jamming, etc.). Everything seems to be working smoothly. I'd recommend this for anyone over 10 yrs old -- old enough to be able to handle a hot glue gun for sure. I'll keep working with it and will post a photo or two if I create something I consider worth sharing. Overall: solid 4 stars -- I like it!

I've said all along that if I could design the perfect coloring book, it would start with compelling designs and be printed on the best paper.

This book comes very close to perfect for me.

The mandala designs offer a wide variety: some very detailed, some more "open" -- 50 designs in all. All are crisply printed, black on white paper, one side only. The book is spiral-bound at the top, making it easy to flip to the image you want to color, and the pages are perforated for removal.

But the paper! Wow! This is heavy smooth card -- very similar to [[ASIN:B015WTANV4 Spectrum Noir Ultra Smooth Premium White Cardstock 8.5x11-]] which I've used quite often in my card-making for stamped images that I then color with alcohol pens. It really works great for any pens: alcohol-based (BIC Mark-its, Sharpies, Copics, Spectrum Noir, etc.), water based (Crayola SuperTips, etc.). It is a little less successful with wax-based colored pencils (Prismacolor), but works nicely with oil-based (FaberCastell Polychromos, Marco Raffine, etc.).

The covers are heavy - almost like chipboard - but lay flat. No complaints there. Really, no complaints at all. I'll be looking for the next edition by this artist/publisher (the cover says "Volume 1," so I assume additional similar books will be released in the future).

Solid 5-stars from me: I love it!

I use Aunt Lydia's size 10 crochet cotton a lot for doilies, coasters, and placemats. And I use a lot of their metallic threads for highlights as well as by itself for holiday projects.

This natural/gold combination works great for Christmas ornaments. It has a subtle gold sheen -- and a hint of sparkle/bling -- that works with a lot of color palettes. I've also used the gold/gold metallic ([[ASIN:B004WQPITQ Coats Crochet Aunt Lydia's Crochet, Cotton Metallic Size 10, Gold/Gold]]), but I actually like this better (not quite as "yellow").

Although this is a size 10 thread, the addition of the metallic thread makes it just a bit bigger -- not enough to make a huge difference in a smaller project, but it might add a little to the diameter of a larger doily or tree skirt, for example. It also adds a bit of "stiffness" to the thread, so it doesn't glide as smoothly as "normal" Aunt Lydia's. Once you get used to it, however, it's not a problem.

I purchased this in a "package" of 3 balls since I use quite a lot of this to make star ornaments for fundraisers. Good value, nice quality. Solid 4 stars.

I absolutely LOVE this yarn. It's soft and supple, and the colors are really nice. I purchased the Parrot variegated -- the colors range from gold to peacock, with some light sage/mint and some darker gray/green sections. I especially like the gold thread running through -- it gives a hint of sparkle. And you get a generous 448 yds per ball and it's machine washable & dryable (it's mostly acrylic), too.

Sizewise, it's a "fine" yarn -- sometimes designated as "superfine" or "lace" weight 1. I'm using it for a lacy scarf, using a size 4.0mm crochet hook. It's easy to work with, consistent quality (no "thin" sections or knots so far), really no complaints at all.

I've just ordered more of this in another color combination ("Berry Smash" -- pinks/purples) to use for another scarf pattern. Highly recommended if you're looking for lace-weight yarn that's soft, easy to work with, and washable.

Still not sure if this is going to work or not. I use the "knife grip" so this looked like a good option. After nearly a week of working with it, I'm not convinced.

I have fairly severe osteoarthritis in my right hand and wrist, and crocheting has gotten almost too painful. I've tried a number of options with varying degrees of success. This works best to mitigate the pain my hand but is NOT working all that well for my wrist.

Something about the way the handle is bent/angled means that I have to "twist" my wrist in order to do certain stitches, and therein lies the problem. That extra twisting motion isn't good for my wrist at all. It's "tweaking" something (probably a bone spur or cyst), so I can only crochet for about 30 minutes before it becomes too painful to continue.

I LOVE the way the metal part is constructed. The "hook" part is just about perfect, right size, right "depth," yarn-overs are a breeze (no snagging, no splitting the thread, etc.). I wish the shank were a little longer, but overall, the shape is ideal.

I had such high hopes for this -- and it looks like it works great for some people. I'd guess that if the problem is primarily in your hand, this might be a good solution. But if you also have wrist problems, it might make it worse. For me, it's just "OK."

I've been a long-time fan of Hot Off the Press products and purchase from their website quite often. I was looking for something a little different to use for some last-minute holiday cards and remembered seeing these. I can get them from Amazon a lot sooner than waiting (and paying extra) for shipping from the source, so I ordered a couple of packs of these.

These make really nice unusual cards. You have a large "panel" on the one side (left or right, depending on how you fold these), and three smaller "step-up" panels on the other side. I ended up making a "snow scene" with the 3 smaller step-up panels, with snowmen, penguins, etc. and used the larger panel for my holiday sentiment. Worked great, and didn't take a lot of time to put together.

You get 5 cards and 5 envelopes, some ideas for how to put them together, and patterns to make the panels with a small matted border. But really, just use your imagination. I'm getting ready to do some cards for spring that will feature a "flower garden" arrangement in the step-up panels.

I make a lot of handcrafted greeting cards and use different types of embellishments: bows, die cuts, small charms, paper flowers, etc. I prefer using a non-liquid adhesive because I don't have to wait for drying.

So I tend to use a lot of "dot"-type adhesives, and I find that Zots are probably the best on the market. With the different sizes offered, I can always find just the right size to attach a small bow or tiny die-cut flower. This 1/8" size gets used a lot for my more delicate embellishments, and 325 dots lasts a long time. Easy to use, too.

Very happy with these, and I'll continue ordering them as long as the price remains reasonable.

I make a lot of handcrafted greeting cards, so I use a paper cutter in every project. I'm on my 3rd "cartridge"-type trimmer, and find them easy to manage and use. So I was very interested in trying a guillotine paper cutter to compare.

I probably won't be switching over to this Martha Stewart Heavy-Duty Paper Trimmer. It does have a few advantages, but they're not enough to overcome the negatives.

Pros:

Accurate cuts -- I found it easier to get a good consistent cut by NOT using the "guide," and instead just lining card stock or paper carefully in the guides printed on the base (see "con" below).

Light weight -- easy to move around

Safety features -- locking mechanism on the handle, finger guard

Small storage compartment for pencil, etc. It's not very big but it doesn't need to be

Cons:
It has a large footprint and takes up a lot more space on my craft table than my current cutter.

I'm still trying to figure out what they mean by "heavy duty." My existing cartridge model is sturdier and just feels like it could last forever (I usually get 4 yrs or more from my cartridge models).

I can cut multiple sheets easier with this Martha Stewart model, BUT the "gripper" that holds the paper down to keep it from shifting doesn't work that well when you're cutting more than a few sheets.

The guide "moves" when you lock it into place, which sort of defeats the purpose. You really have to pay attention and hold it in place as you turn the lock.

It takes a lot more arm movement to use the guillotine blade than it does to use the cartridge trimmer. This may not matter to some, but I have severe arthritis in my shoulder, and had some serious pain issues after working on a set of greeting cards (maybe 40 or so "cuts"). I'll keep working with it, but I'll have to find another way to use it that doesn't "tweek" my shoulder so much.

I'm really wondering how long the blade will remain sharp. I've used it for the past 10 days on a series of cards for a fund raiser and I'm starting to get some "fuzzy" cuts in one section of the blade just on a single sheet of good card stock. With my current cartridge model, I would just replace the blade and get back to work. A blade usually lasts 4 months with the kind and amount of work I do with it. So that's a concern.

Overall, this is an "OK" product. Not great, not terrible, but I honestly think there are better options out there in home hobby guillotine cutters. I'd recommend finding one in a store somewhere and see for yourself if it's something that will enhance your crafting experience.

This set seems to work best for more contemporary style cards. The type style is wonderful -- quirky and casual. I often make sets of cards to sell at various fundraising events, so having a variety of sentiments in the same or similar type style helps to unify the cards in a set (along with matching colors/layouts/etc.). This is the one I use when I'm doing more "clean and simple" cards (as opposed to my "fancy/bling/ribbons/lace" cards).

Paper Smooches tends to specialize in this more whimsical style of stamps/etc., and the quality it always good. I own several of their stamp & die sets -- never had a problem.

I've used all of these so far to make some holiday cards for a fundraiser coming up later this summer, and I really do like them.

They ARE a bit trickier than some of the other Spellbinders dies, even some of the intricate lacy ones. I've gotten them to work by using wax paper between the die and my paper/cardstock, and also by using a ball stylus tool (from a flower shaper set) that will fit the release holes in each die. They cut nicely, but I usually use a metal shim just to be sure I'll get a clean cut.

I've been using these with mirror card stock in gold and silver, as well as textured solid color card stock, and so far, I like the results.

I like these; don't love them, but can recommend to those who don't mind putting a bit more effort into working with them.

I make handcrafted greeting cards, and often use embellishments similar to these as flower centers. These are just lovely: pearls and clear rhinestones set in silver-toned metal. They're larger than some -- about 5/8" in diameter -- so bear that in mind. Also, if you're planning to mail a card you make with these, you'll need to package it in a "puffy" envelope; the post office won't process it otherwise due to the thickness of the embellishment.

Still, they're beautiful stick-on bits of bling that add a special touch of class to my cards, and I definitely will be ordering more.

I have several sets of these Prima "Say It In ... " sets (Say It In Pearls, Say It In Studs, etc.).  They're all just gorgeous!  The work very nicely as flower centers for some larger punched flowers.  Easy to use, just remove the adhesive clusters from the backing and apply it to your project.  That simple!  The colors of this set are great for the fall cards I'm working on right now: a medium olive/sage-y green, off-white, black, and a tiny bit of silvery gray.  You get a dozen of them, and a little goes a long way.  Very nice!

I purchased several colors of this to use on my handcrafted greeting cards, and I couldn't be happier.

It has a soft feel, is relatively lightweight, and is very easy to manipulate. I try to keep the "lumps and bumps" to a minimum on my cards, as the postal service tends to object to items that won't go easily through the sorter. This ribbon allows me to make a nice "fluffy" bow without a huge knot to get in the way.

The width is perfect, too, for my use. I got Buttercream, Celery, Red, and White -- and I'll be using most of those for my fall and winter cards this year.

You get quite a lot for the price, too -- so it's reasonably economical. VERY happy with this!

I'm not sure why the description says "2-pack" and "two nested dies in one 9 x 5 1/4 in package." There is only ** 1 ** die in the package, and it is stunning.

This is in the Amazing Paper Grace collection of JustRite/Spellbinders dies. Becca Feeken, the talent behind Amazing Paper Grace, has a style that is instantly recognizable: opulent elegance, beautiful lines, lots of lace and intricate die cuts. This is Heirloom Flourish One (there's an Heirloom Flourish Two, also), and the cut piece is 3 3/4" by 1 1/2" tip-to-tip and side-to-side. It's just the right size to provide a lovely accent piece for a card (A2 or larger), or on a scrapbook page.

The quality is the usual Spellbinders quality -- nice sharp edges, and no problem using this with a Sizzix Big Shot. I use a couple of pieces of card stock as a shim with these intricate dies to ensure a nice clean cut.

Overall, I'm very satisfied with this die. I'll upload a photo of the actual die itself so you can see how it's constructed (one single die, NOT TWO!).

I'm a huge fan of Penny Black stamps. Their quality is always first-rate, and this set is no exception.

I purchased these to use on cards I find myself making more and more these days: cards for friends who have lost a loved one, or for friends who are facing (usually) serious health challenges. The type styles are classy, the sentiments are subdued but meaningful, and they're the perfect size for my cards.

I usually stamp these in Versamark and then heat emboss in a color or metallic fine embossing powder. Combined with a delicate die-cut flower spray and a bit of color, I can create a lovely card in no time.

The little flowers in the set come in handy, too, for use with other stamps. Definitely worth the investment, I'm getting a lot of use from this lovely set.

I was quite happy with this.  Nice colors, nice quality.  It is actually a decent quality crochet-look lace with adhesive.  My only quibble is that you don't get very much of it in each roll (less than 3 yds), but it works fine for the cards I like to make.  I would purchase again as long as the price stays reasonable.

... despite what the photo shows. These are more the color of tarnished brass. They'll be usable for my purpose, but if having true gold-colored rings is important, look elsewhere. I found more "real" gold colored rings in the [[ASIN:B00HUKV3EK 15MM (.59in.) Aluminum Jump Rings (Assorted Darks, 72 pieces/bag)]] package (along with black, pewter, red, brown, etc.).

Quality seems to be fine; just the color is way off from the description.

I purchased this to make "foliage" to go with the flowers that I use often on hand-crafted greeting cards. The fern itself is a good size, just under 2" long (the dimensions given in the product description are for the PUNCH itself, NOT the punched image -- don't you hate that??).

The primary problem I've had with this is that it can be difficult to punch. You really have to place it flat on a hard surface, and "lean" into it with both hands to get it to punch through anything. I've tried running aluminum foil and waxed paper through it, and it doesn't seem to help much. It's loosened up a bit just from use, but be prepared when you first get it that it will be difficult to punch. This has been a common problem with these Punch Bunch brand punches due to the design: a button rather than a lever. Punch Bunch even makes a lever mechanism to solve this problem but it's pricey [[ASIN:B004QIKXHC Punch Bunch Magnum Power Punch Aide]] .

That said, it punches cleanly, no ragged edges, and it suits my purpose just fine. I think it's worth 4 stars (I do like it), but not 5 due to the "ease of use" issues.

I needed some new papers to play with for spring cards, and saw this on a designer's blog. I fell in love with the colors and patterns.

As a card maker, I look for patterns that aren't *too* large, that will work well in the smaller format of 4.25" x 5.5" or 5" x 6.5" - the sizes I make most of the time.

I also have a lot of yellow, green, navy, and pink solid card stock on hand, that match this palette perfectly. I've already got several card packs planned using this set of coordinated papers.

Very nice! Highly recommend!

I make hand-crafted greeting cards, and purchased this to use with this year's batch of Christmas cards. The colors are vibrant, the quality is excellent, and you get a nice selection of patterns and prints.

Predominant colors are red, brown, white, and green -- and the shades are easy to match with card stock from other manufacturers, as well as easy to match with ribbons and other trims.

I like the "folksy" theme of this set, too -- not *too* fancy, but there is a lot of flexibility as to how formal or casual you want to go.

Very pleased with this!

I love these layered flowers! You get two sheets of die-cut flowers + centers (only one sheet is shown above) of different sizes and shapes that you can layer using foam dots or glue dots to form multi-layer flowers for your cards or scrapbook pages.

The colors are a perfect match for the [[ASIN:B00AZGJ7TO Graphic 45 Secret Garden Pad, 12 by 12-Inch]], or any of the other Secret Garden paper pads. There are so many ways to use these: layered (as mentioned above), or single flowers added to an envelope or card inside.

I love the combination of peachy-pinks, soft blues and lilacs, soft yellows -- really nice versatile colors.

The quality of the paper is excellent (it's Graphic 45 -- their products are always good quality), and they punch cleanly with very small "nibs" that are easy to remove with a fingernail.

Very happy with these.

Why did I wait so long? I've been reading about this stuff for several months, and finally decided to give it a try. I needed something that would adhere microbeads for flower centers without showing, and this seemed perfect.

Well, it IS! I've used it already in several cards to hold [[ASIN:B009D1ZESU 1oz = 28g Plastic Microbeads Nohole 0.6mm Transparent Sunglow Yellow]] in my daisies, but I'm also using it by itself to highlight sections of flowers, birds, butterflies. A thin layer makes a really nice transparent shiny accent, and the needle-size tip makes accurate placement easy.

It doesn't take a lot, either -- really just a tiny amount goes a LONG way. Very happy with this!

I purchased my first squeeze punch corner punch [[ASIN:B0018BWIYG Fiskars FCSP-7334 Corner Squeeze Paper Punch, Something Old, Something New]] several weeks ago, and liked it so much that I came back to get this one.

I have arthritis in my hands, and a lot of the traditional style punches are just too difficult to use. These squeeze punches seem to have solved that problem, as i find I can use these with no discomfort at all: The amount of effort needed to punch even heavy card stock (110#) isn't much.

I really like that there are three different designs in one punch. There's a "dial" that you turn to dial in the desired design -- be careful to line it up just right, or your punch will be crooked. So, for the price of a single punch, you get three. Nice!

I'm very happy with this. I've used it several times already on my cards with good results.

I've been contemplating purchasing these as separate sets for awhile, and when I found them as a "package" deal here on Amazon, I decided to go for it.

You get 4 coordinated sets of dies. The circle die sets contain 7 dies each, and between the two sets, you can do matting with 1/8" borders. The scalloped circle sets contain 6 dies each, and again, you can do 1/8" matting. But when you combine the two sets, you get a ton of matting possibilities. Layer a circle on a scalloped circle; layer a scallop circle on top of a "plain" circle -- with varying border widths.

I use magnetic sheets to store these ([[ASIN:B007Q41MO6 Spellbinder Magnetic Die Case]] combined with [[ASIN:B007SOV4JM ArtBin 6979AB Magnetic Die Sheets, 3-Pack]] -- I use a separate case for my "shapes" nestabilities-style dies (circles, ovals, flags, tags, etc.), so they're always handy and ready to use.

At the time I purchased these (February 2013), the price was excellent for the 4 sets. Definitely happy with my purchase.

As others have mentioned, the colors in this twine aren't super bright -- that's why I ordered it. I needed more subtle colors for my projects. I make handcrafted greeting cards, and use twine as a trim. It's the perfect size to use with small buttons, and to wrap around a focal without taking up too much real estate. It's about the same diameter as 6-strand embroidery floss.

Very happy with this -- perfect for my use.

Very nice stamps, and you get a lot for the money.

These coordinate with Spellbinders dies [[ASIN:B005CZNFMS Spellbinders 2011 Heirloom Ornaments]] and [[ASIN:B00427TQY8 Spellbinders Shapeabilities 2010 Christmas Heirloom ornament Die Templates]]. I love that the "frames" are separate from the "insides," so you can interchange them to get dozens of combinations.

I stamp the frame in one color on white card stock, the message in another color, then use my [[ASIN:B001AGZZIU Sizzix 655268 Big Shot Cutting-and-Embossing Roller-Style Machine]] to cut out the stamped image. I then matte on a coordinating color before attaching to my card.

Very nice set of sentiments and shapes, even without the Spellbinders dies.

I have quite a few of these Spellbinders folders, and I've been using this Regal folder to emboss cardstock that I've been using for Christmas cards. They work great with my Sizzix Big Shot embossing/die cutting machine. Just follow the directions for layering -- these are just a bit "thicker" than the Sizzix folders

The best part of these Spellbinders M-Bossibilities is that you get two embossing patterns in one folder. You simply reverse the fold, and you have a different pattern. It's like getting two folders for the price of one!

Very happy with this one -- the two patterns are very elegant, and suitable for lots of different occasions.

I purchased these for my [[ASIN:B0017KYE5Y Fiskars 196920-1001 Portable Paper Trimmer, 12-Inch]], and they fit just fine. I make a lot of greeting cards, and use y trimmer a lot, but these blades seem to last a long time. I make sure to use them in both directions.

Be sure to check carefully to make sure you order the correct blades. They all look similar, but some are "triple track," and some aren't.

I love these stickers! They look like stamped/heat embossed designs, but they're peel-and-stick.

I use these for cards, and they're super easy to use. One tip: use tweezers to help align the sticker -- much easier than just using fingers.

Also watch the pricing on these. Other sites will occasionally have them cheaper. Still, for the number of stickers you get, Amazon's price isn't too out of line.

I do a lot of card crafting, and use a fair amount of foam tape to create texture and dimension. I don't like my foam tape to be *too* thick, as I sometimes layer two or three pieces to create the effect I want. I've been using 1/16"-thick tape, but I've been searching for something just a bit thinner.

This tape is just right. It's slightly thinner than 1/16", which allows me to layer more "bits" without creating a card that is unwieldy. I can still use my thicker tapes when I have only one layer. It's easy to manipulate, too -- I sometimes cut it in half when i need narrower tape for projects (papier tole, for example), and it performs beautifully. Will definitely keep this on hand!
Works great for my messy oil painting studio. I cover the table top with it. Absorbent with oil paints, solvent, etc. No bleed thru.

This powerloc roadbed track is a great track for kids and beginners. For the kids the no joiner Assembly makes grades, or hills easier to make, they also wont fall apart like the Bachmann E-Z track if there picked up with-out separating them. They can also get very dirty and still work well. I recommend the nickle silver for modelers. I recommend the steel(The one thats Black, not Grey) for the kids because its cheap and easily replaceable if broken, or stepped on(ouch!). This is the first inner circle by-the-way you can create two running circles of track with the 22" radius track to simulate a double track line(also allows two trains to run at once all-be-it on separate tracks). If your looking for good model quality track I suggest Atlas roadbed track.

Before I leave any negative feedback I always read the item description in full to see if I’ve missed anything. I did not. This says it’s “polyester”. If it was polyester as described the ends would melt when burned, as I needed it to do for a knitting project. So my guess is it’s some cotton blend. That would be doable if the cord was the same color on the inside that it is in the outside like every piece of wrapped cord I’ve ever used before. It’s not! It’s all wrapped in the ugly blue obviously cotton thread so one cut and unravels it’s not e.g. green any longer it’s green and about the color of dull blue of cheap furniture blankets. Very unattractive! So I though okay I can’t use it as knotting so I’ll sew it on to something. NO! One can’t do that either as when the machine stitches over it it pulls up these ugly blue and or grey interior fiber. So if you’re expecting a solid color strand in satin polyester, that’s NOT what you’re getting. I could have bought four times as much of solid satin polyester thread locally that was far superior quality. Very disappointed at this investment.

This makes me nauseous to look through. I am not new to magnifying glass use. I have a few, some lighted some not. I keep one at my desk, one in kitchen, one on second floor of house. When I move the glass away at any distance, the distortion is vomit inducing. I can make out the words, but it isnt right. Again NONE of the others I have do this. I can use them up close or from a ways away. No issues. This? not usable as a magnifying glass. Generous 2 stars

Good weight, but might be too heft for frail folks. If you can handle the weight, then it will feel good in hand. Feels durable. Quality seems fine minus the whole makes me want to vomit thing. Ah well.

Goodness, this is a gorgeous design. If you are here looking at this WAX material, then you already know what the material feels like. It has been a long time since I handled wax material. I used to collect figurines using the Batik wax coloring method (Indonesian wax-resist dyeing technique). When it comes to softer materials, as far as I knew it was pure cotton that would be infused with the wax (making it stiff) and the coloring process. This is polyester, and the color is rich on both sides (rather than rich on one, dull on other). But the overall feel of the material is exactly what I was expecting.. so I give it a favorable rating.

Also, it has other hallmarks that one should look for, such as seeing the fabric company, product and registration number printed on the selvage of the fabric. "Guaranteed Realwax Block 20FP6067"

Realwax Industries, a textile business is located in India with its focused main market : North America, South America, Eastern Europe, Africa, Mid East

If you are new to it, please know that wax material is stiff. When used to make clothing and costumes, it provides a great amount of structure. It is typically used in the creation of formal wear. It is worth reading the history of the material to gain a better understanding of why it was/is used and what it looks/feels like.

Overall-- gorgeous, bright, bold, dense, firm material that can be used for variety of uses. The amount of material is generous, and it should accommodate most any product you have in mind. Size: Length : 6 yds ( 216 inch). Width: 45 inch

"Care: We strongly recommend washing by hand in not too much water hot to preserve the fabric. But if you choose the machine wash, do not exceed 30 °c"

The main large sticker comes off with ease, and leaves no stickiness behind. However, the "realwax Guarantee" seal is pretty well solidly on there. It does appear to pick off some color as it is peeled back. On the plus side, it is along the selvage (edge) of the fabric and shouldn't impede any projects. The stickiness/outline left behind is small, and can be carefully worked off with some warm water and a rag.

While I HAD only thought of Ankara wax material as being made with 100% cotton (traditionally it was), I have learned through testing/reviewing this product that the use of polyester instead is also greatly used, and is a much more budget friendly option.

5 stars for me!

I have minimal to no printing needs, but I usually keep a pack of white cardstock on hand. It is simply a versatile item for uses beyond printing. I love to doodle on card stock with markers, often a simple black shaprie marker pen. I tend to like it better than sketch books when I use those black ultra fine markers. I like the firm feel of the "paper" and that there is no bleed through.

Generalize that to making lists, charts, small posters, and even the occasional hand made card or for use in other organizing projects, it just keeps me maintaining my cardstock stock.

24 small tubes (12ml, or .4 ounces) of a wide variety of colors. I am just trying to get back into some creative projects, and I thought I would refresh my stash of paint. If I knew I would be getting into bigger projects, I would buy larger containers. When I saw that this was a set of small tubes, I thought it would be perfect for me as I didn't want to any to go to waste. All of the tubes in my package look good. I pressed all of them, and they feel soft. I opened up half dozen, and those were all fine. Hopefully remainder are good.

I think this is a nice kit to have if you want to dabble, or as an add on to a themed gift.

Definitely accurate. This ring sizer will help you. I have a ring that I know the size of and it corresponds to the ringer sizer tool. 23 sizes, from 2-13, including 1/2 size. I like that it is a ring of material rather than a measuring tape of the sort. I have made mistakes with the measuring tape style. The ring style actually provides a feel of a ring on, how it will sit, and what it is like to take it on and off. A measuring style tape just can't provide that- and I have found there is too much room for error.

The aspect that I don't care for is that the rings would be easy to lose or annoying to sort through once they are popped out of the plastic. Some ring sizer tools have metal rings of various sizes that are attached to a larger ring, so they all stay together (for a lot less). While you could slip something through all these rings to hold them together, you would have to take them off any time you want to try one on. I was thinking that maybe I could just keep them attached, but my finger kinda got stuck trying a smaller size, and I pulled the ring right off the card. I can see the size on the tab, but I suspect some people may have difficulty seeing it. I do not know how that compares to other ring size tools.

So yes, I am happy to have this, as I keep forgetting my ring size, particularly differences between hands. I sometimes see a cute cheap ring I want to buy then I cant recall sizes of each finger. "Love Spell". Smells so nice! I am so pleased that there are products like these available. I enjoy using a wax warmer on occasion- makes the room feel cozy, but I don't care for the usual wax scents that are offered in stores. I am prone to scent nausea, and so many are just headache triggers. Not just from the scent but also the strength of the scent. I noticed that some reviews have mentioned that there is minimal fragrance output. I am curious if those reviewers are used to the typical scented wax offered in most stores? Those are really strong, so much so it is hard to have a wax warmer in same room due to overwhelming strength of scent. If you are used to that, then it is important to know that these style of scented wax are much more subtle. It's there, but can be hard to tell if you are used to the other kinds. If you need super strong scents, then just grab something off the shelf. These soy wax with pure essential oils are subtle yet aromatic. I love the way they gently fill the room with subtle fragrance. Stronger when close by of course, but still hints of it away from wax burner. I have no issue with nausea or head aches when I use these. Such a cozy light smell.
I have used other brands, that are similar in make up, and have the exact same thing to say. I don't think any of the soy/bees wax essential oil wax warmers are less than 5 stars.

It is thin which makes it so comfortable when working on my canvas. My old one was thicker and sometimes it hurt to rest my arm on it. So I love the size of this one.

Another plus is that it doesn't get hot. I have had a couple of light pads that got really hot after an hour. I used this one for around 3 hours and it did not get super hot.

I also love the size but hope to get the bigger one soon. A lot of my paintings are 30x40 or 40x50, so this was great for me. However, if you work with really large paintings I would get the bigger one.

The only small complaints I have is- it is really easy to turn it off if your arm hits the power button. It is not a huge hindrance but pretty annoying. Also, I wish the USB cord was longer, but that is an easy fix. I just bought one that was longer.

All in all I am super pleased with the quality and will definitely return to purchase the larger light pad at a later date!
